citations.narrative:
During the inspection, the licensee interfered with the inspector’s ability to fully evaluate the care of the 8-10 month-old
tiger for compliance with the Animal Welfare Regulations. The inspectors asked the licensee about the growing animal's
diet and calcium supplementation. The licensee refused to describe or demonstrate to inspectors the amount of calcium
supplementation that was being provided. The licensee described adding 1/2 a "spoonful" of Fresh Oasis Feline T
supplement to ground meat (not utilizing the provided scoop, nor following the administration guidelines on the product).
The licensee provided a prepackaged portion of ground beef as the meat being fed, but was unwilling to describe
frequency and amount, other than stating, “it’s a 3-pound package.” and walking into the house.
Inspectors provided information regarding the importance of calcium for nondomestic felids and discussed the need to
evaluate the animal moving to potentially identify gait abnormalities associated with metabolic bone disease. Although the
licensee allowed the inspectors a brief view of the tiger and the room it was being housed in by peeking around the
partially opened door, each inspector was only allowed to view the animal for 10 to 20 seconds, observing the animal
lying in left lateral recumbency, before being told that was enough by the licensee. Inspectors asked multiple times,
offering different viewing options to see the cat move, and the licensee refused all options including video or observation
from the window by opening the curtains on the door. After the licensee refused, he entered the room with the tiger and
shut the door behind him, denying inspectors the opportunity for further discussion and visualization of the tiger.
A facility representative repeated that the inspectors would not be allowed to see the tiger, the vet would evaluate
tomorrow, she had other things to do, and she asked the inspectors to leave the facility. The inspectors communicated
that the facility’s refusal to even attempt to allow inspectors an avenue to thoroughly observe the animal interfered with
their ability to carry out their duties. She told the inspectors that attending veterinarian would be at the facility tomorrow,
that the inspectors could rely on the AV's evaluation, and that the inspectors could not go into the room to evaluate the
tiger because the facility representative herself did not even go in. Inspectors repeated that the inspectors needed to see
the animal to assess compliance, and the facility representative replied that the inspectors had been at the facility "a long
time" and she had other things to do. The inspectors had to end the inspection without assessing the tiger.
Interfering with APHIS officials in the course of their official duties prevents officials from effectively ensuring the humane
care and treatment of animals as required by the Animal Welfare Regulations.
Licensees shall not interfere with, threaten, abuse (including verbally abuse), or harass any APHIS official in the course of
carrying out his or her duties.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
The licensee has a part time attending veterinarian located in Reno, NV and a written Program of Veterinary Care, but
does not have arrangements for the Attending Veterinarian (AV) to conduct regularly scheduled visits to the premises.
The licensee provided a Program of Veterinary Care with the space documenting the frequency of the regularly scheduled
visits by the veterinarian left blank. The licensee could not provide specific information about any regularly scheduled
visits to the facility by the Attending Veterinarian in the last two years. APHIS inspectors called the AV and determined
that the AV last visited the premises in July 2024 for a tiger with a health problem.
Without the required regularly scheduled visits to the premises, the AV cannot effectively ensure the provision of
adequate veterinary care and oversee the adequacy of other aspects of animal care and use.
Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor.

citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Since the Attending Veterinarian’s (AV) last visit to the facility in July of 2024, a new tiger cub was acquired in October of
2025 that has not been examined by the AV, nor has the animal’s diet, medical care, and handling been discussed with
the AV. The licensee called another veterinarian and reported that the tiger had constipation and lameness. That
veterinarian visited the facility and saw the tiger at their clinic in November 2025, but the licensee did not follow the
treatment plan prescribed by that veterinarian and did not report the concerns to the AV.
The other veterinarian's records also state that the licensee left a message on April 20, 2026 that "P has re-sprained his
ankle and he has not gone to the bathroom in 4 days." The licensee made an appointment, which he later cancelled
because he said that the animal defecated and he had no other concerns. The licensee did not report any of these
problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being to the AV.
Failure to directly and frequently communicate problems regarding animal health can delay proper care.
Each dealer or exhibitor shall establish and maintain programs of adequate veterinary care that include daily observation
of all animals to assess their health and well-being: Provided, however, that daily observation of animals may be
accomplished by someone other than the attending veterinarian and provided further, that a mechanism of direct and
frequent communication is required so that timely and accurate information on problems of animal health, behavior and
well being is conveyed to the attending veterinarian.

An 8-10 month old tiger was housed in a room at the back of the residence, with the only entry/exit door opened directly
outside. Within the room, one wall was made of shingles, there was a wood floor and boarded up windows with plywood.
The space contained a television, space heater and air conditioner, all of which were plugged in and accessible to the
tiger at the time of inspection. Other items within the room and accessible to the tiger included fabric couches, fabric
pillows, plastic airline crates with various towels and other fabrics, empty water bottles, dish soap, and various other
items.
Housing a tiger in a room with furniture, fabrics, plugged in electric appliances and other items could result in injury to the
animal.
The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the
animals from injury and to contain the animals.
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the
applicant.
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by 19 July, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection.
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires on 10 June 2026.
Additional Inspectors:
William Campbell, VETERINARY MEDICAL OFFICER n

narrative:
2.4  Critical
   
Non-interference with APHIS officials. 
During the inspection, the licensee interfered with the inspector’s ability to fully evaluate the care of the 8-10 month-old

Licensees shall not interfere with, threaten, abuse (including verbally abuse), or harass any APHIS official in the course of 
carrying out his or her duties. 
2.40(a)(1) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
The licensee has a part time attending veterinarian located in Reno, NV and a written Program of Veterinary Care, but 
does not have arrangements for the Attending Veterinarian (AV) to conduct regularly scheduled visits to the premises.

Each dealer and exhibitor shall employ an attending veterinarian under formal arrangements. In the case of a part-time 
attending veterinarian or consultant arrangements, the formal arrangements shall include a written program of veterinary 
care and regularly scheduled visits to the premises of the dealer or exhibitor. 
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Since the Attending Veterinarian’s (AV) last visit to the facility in July of 2024, a new tiger cub was acquired in October of 
2025 that has not been examined by the AV, nor has the animal’s diet, medical care, and handling been discussed with

3.125(a) 
   
Facilities, general. 
An 8-10 month old tiger was housed in a room at the back of the residence, with the only entry/exit door opened directly 
outside. Within the room, one wall was made of shingles, there was a wood floor and boarded up windows with plywood. 
The space contained a television, space heater and air conditioner, all of which were plugged in and accessible to the 
tiger at the time of inspection. Other items within the room and accessible to the tiger included fabric couches, fabric 
pillows, plastic airline crates with various towels and other fabrics, empty water bottles, dish soap, and various other 
items.  
 
Housing a tiger in a room with furniture, fabrics, plugged in electric appliances and other items could result in injury to the 
animal. 
 
The indoor and outdoor housing facilities shall be structurally sound and shall be maintained in good repair to protect the 
animals from injury and to contain the animals. 
 
This is a 1st Re-License Inspection for a Class “C” license. The inspection and exit briefing were conducted with the 
applicant. 
 
All items must be in compliance within 2 more inspections or by 19 July, 2026 or the applicant will forfeit the license fee 
and must wait 6 months to reapply. Please contact your inspector to schedule your next Re-License Inspection. 
 
Regulated activity can continue under your existing USDA license until it expires on 10 June 2026.
